text: The surgeon performs a laparoscopic Whipple procedure with the patient positioned supine and legs spread. Multiple ports are used for access. Fat between the colon and the lower border of the stomach is divided, revealing the pink vascular tissue of the stomach. The right colon is mobilized to clear the surgical field. The stomach is lifted off the pancreas, creating a tunnel behind the stomach. Fatty tissue surrounding veins and a small artery near the stomach is dissected. A stapler is used to take the gastropoploic vessels. The stomach is divided to allow better exposure of the pancreas for resection of the duodenum.

The surgeon dissects around the lymph node on the artery leading to the liver, preserving the hepatic artery while identifying the artery running to the duodenum and pancreas for division. The dissection continues across the duodenum to mobilize it from the porta hepatis. The gastroduodenal artery is visible and needs to be divided. The hepatic artery is elevated with an instrument, revealing the portal vein and common bile duct behind it.

The surgeon carefully teases the bile duct off the portal vein and passes an instrument behind it. The connection of the gallbladder to the barlet is divided, revealing the long cystic artery. A stapler is used to divide the barlet, preventing bleeding from the cut edge. The focus shifts to the area below the pancreas, where a tunnel is defined underneath the pancreatic neck, which runs over the superior mesenteric vein.

The surgeon lifts the pancreatic neck forward and uses a harmonic scalpel to dissect and control small blood vessels. The superior mesenteric vein is visible, and the pancreas is carefully teased off the vein, creating a tunnel underneath. A tape is passed around the neck of the pancreas to lift it and apply tension for further dissection alongside the vein.

The surgeon opens a plane between the bowel fat, particularly the colon, and the head of the pancreas, where the tumor is located. A bloodless plane is visible alongside a vein, aiding in the separation of the colon from the pancreas. As the dissection progresses, the plane leads to a separation point between the duodenum and the colon. The duodenum is grasped, and the colon fat is teased away from it, allowing further dissection. The assistant uses force as a retractor to pull the bowel down, utilizing the previously prepared space around the colon.

The surgeon performs a cocker maneuver, rolling the duodenum away from the posterior abdominal structures. The duodenum is elevated from the fatty tissue of the bowel and the inferior vena cava, dividing loose tissue attachments without blood supply. The duodenum is pulled from beneath the superior mesenteric artery and vein, revealing the aorta and continuing dissection along the front of the inferior vena cava towards the liver.

The surgeon dissects the left renal vein as it crosses the aorta, just below the origin of the superior mesenteric artery. The vein is visible in the surgical field, with the duodenum pushed to the right-hand side of the view. Lymph nodes between the aorta and inferior vena cava are sampled and placed in a bag. The inferior vena cava, left renal vein, and aorta are clearly visible.

The surgeon mobilizes the left renal vein and continues to pull the duodenum through, creating a visible window from the opposite side. The duodenum is pulled across to the left, revealing the head of the pancreas in the surrounding fatty tissue. A small vein running from the head of the pancreas to the superior mesenteric vein is divided. The bowel is opened and divided at the duodenal jejunal flexure using a stapling device, with the tissue appearing purple due to the loss of blood supply. The duodenum and pancreas are lifted away from the vessels running to the bowel, enhancing mobility for further dissection.

The surgeon dissects along the artery, taking care to manage branches, including one large main branch and several smaller ones. The uncinate process is lifted off the bowel mesentery, and the head of the pancreas is elevated. The neck of the pancreas is divided at the point where it crosses in front of the vein using a harmonic scalpel to seal small blood vessels. Upon reaching the pancreatic duct, scissors are used to divide it, revealing the clear pancreatic juice. The remaining pancreas is then divided with the harmonic scalpel.

The surgeon cuts a small piece from the edge of the pancreatic duct specimen for a frozen section analysis. The dissection continues along the artery and in front of the vein, with the inferior pancreaticoduodenal artery and tributaries to the superior mesenteric vein and portal vein being clipped and divided. The surgeon aims to remove lymphatic tissue from behind the portal vein, using a sucker positioned under the hepatic artery, with the gastroduodenal artery clipped.

The surgeon retracts the vein using a sucker to remove the remaining lymph nodes. The inferior vena cava, staple bile duct, gastroduodenal artery, and portal vein are visible. The specimen is placed in a plastic bag for later removal. The staple line is cut away, revealing a healthy blood supply to the bile duct, which is temporarily clamped. The bowel is brought through in preparation for joining the bile duct and pancreas duct. A small hole is made in the bowel, gently opened with forceps, and the back wall is stitched first, followed by the front wall.

The surgeon carefully sews the bile duct and pancreas duct joins, utilizing the enhanced view provided by laparoscopic operation. After completing the back wall, the suture is brought out but not tied immediately. The assistant follows the stitch to maintain tension and prevent snags. Once the front wall is completed, the front wall stitch is tied to the back wall stitch, securing the join.

The surgeon inserts a plastic stent into the pancreatic duct to aid in manipulation and protect the duct during stitching. The pancreas appears highly vascular, with bleeding controlled as stitches are pulled through. A continuous suture is placed along the back wall, creating a barrier. A small hole is opened in the bowel, similar to the bile duct join, to begin placing sutures.

The surgeon performs the pancreatic duct anastomosis using interrupted dissolving sutures, placing eight or nine sutures due to the relatively large duct size. Sutures are strategically placed on the back wall and corners, while a stent is passed from the pancreas side into the bowel side to ensure a secure closure without narrowing the join.

The surgeon places but does not tie the front stitches of the pancreas and bowel join, using a continuous permanent suture to secure the connection and minimize the risk of leakage. The prepared length of bowel is then joined to the stomach using a stapled technique. A small hole is made in both the stomach and bowel to pass the stapler, which creates a vertical line of staples forming the posterior join, leaving a visible white line inside. The hole at the front is subsequently closed.

The surgeon places a corner stitch on one side, aiming to align it with a stitch from the opposite side. A new stitch with barbs is introduced, which locks in place without needing knots, preventing loosening. The sutures are placed continuously, incorporating the staple line to ensure they penetrate healthy tissue beyond the staple line. Once the stitching is complete, the sutures are cut out.

The surgeon removes the gallbladder, which was previously used to lift the liver during the bile duct anastomosis. The gallbladder is placed in a retrieval bag. Drains are positioned around the surgical joins to remove any residual blood or fluid, and the area is irrigated.
